content: Diabetic Nerve Problems Also called: Diabetic neuropathy On this page Basics Summary Start Here Symptoms Diagnosis and Tests Treatments and Therapies Learn More Related Issues Specifics See, Play and Learn No links available Research Clinical Trials Journal Articles Resources Find an Expert For You Patient Handouts Summary If you have diabetes , your blood glucose, or blood sugar , levels are too high. Over time, this can damage the covering on your nerves or the blood vessels that bring oxygen to your nerves. Damaged nerves may stop sending messages, or may send messages slowly or at the wrong times. This damage is called diabetic neuropathy. Over half of people with diabetes get it. Symptoms may include: Numbness in your hands, legs, or feet Shooting pains, burning, or tingling Nausea, vomiting, constipation, or diarrhea Problems with sexual function Urinary problems Dizziness when you change positions quickly Your doctor will diagnose diabetic neuropathy with a physical exam and nerve tests. Controlling your blood sugar can help prevent nerve problems, or keep them from getting worse. Treatment may include pain relief and other medicines.
